section.title: Abijam, Asa, and Baasha
section.sectionRationale: Chapter 15 moves through four reigns in quick succession using the historian's regular regnal formula, each king introduced with his accession year relative to the other kingdom's monarch, his mother's name, the length of his reign, and a verdict on whether he did right or evil in the LORD's eyes. Abijam walks in his father Rehoboam's sins, yet the LORD preserves David's line for the sake of His promise. Asa, by contrast, does what is right, removing the male shrine prostitutes and his own grandmother's idol, though the high places remain and his heart, unlike hers, is fully devoted to the LORD all his days. War with Baasha of Israel drives Asa to purchase Aram's help with temple silver rather than trust the LORD outright. Meanwhile Nadab and then Baasha rise and fall in Israel, each measured against Jeroboam's sin, showing that the LORD's word through Ahijah about Jeroboam's house is fulfilled exactly.
section.thesis: Even as one king after another is measured by the same unbending standard, faithfulness to the LORD as David once showed it, the LORD both judges the unfaithful house of Jeroboam to the last man, exactly as He had promised through Ahijah, and preserves David's line in Judah for His own name's sake, giving Asa space to do what is right even though the reform remains incomplete.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Abijam's brief and unfaithful reign in Judah, verses 1 to 8; Asa's reign and his reforms, verses 9 to 15; the war between Asa and Baasha and Asa's treaty with Aram, verses 16 to 22; the close of Asa's reign, verses 23 to 24; and Nadab and Baasha's reigns in Israel, fulfilling Ahijah's word against Jeroboam's house, verses 25 to 34.

units.label: Abijam reigns in Judah
units.note: a heart not like David's, yet a line preserved

verses.text: In the eighteenth year of the reign of Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abijam became king of Judah,
and he reigned in Jerusalem three years. His mother’s name was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
And Abijam walked in all the sins that his father before him had committed, and his {{lev|heart}} was not as fully devoted to the LORD his God as the heart of David his forefather had been.
Nevertheless, for the sake of David, the LORD his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem by raising up a son to succeed him and to make Jerusalem strong.
For David had done what was right in the eyes of the LORD and had not turned aside from anything the LORD commanded all the days of his life, except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite.
And there was war between the houses of Rehoboam and Jeroboam all the days of Abijam’s life.
As for the rest of the acts of Abijam, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah? And there was war between Abijam and Jeroboam.
And Abijam rested with his fathers and was buried in the City of David, and his son Asa reigned in his place.
In the twentieth year of Jeroboam’s reign over Israel, Asa became king of Judah,
and he reigned in Jerusalem forty-one years. His grandmother’s name was Maacah daughter of Abishalom.
And Asa did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, as his father David had done.
He banished the male shrine prostitutes from the land and removed all the idols that his fathers had made.
He also removed his grandmother Maacah from her position as queen mother because she had made a detestable Asherah pole. Asa chopped down the pole and burned it in the Kidron Valley.
The {{bamah|high places}} were not removed, but Asa’s heart was fully devoted to the LORD all his days.
And he brought into the house of the LORD the silver and gold and the articles that he and his father had dedicated.

units.label: War between Asa and Baasha
units.note: temple treasure buys a treaty with Aram

verses.text: Now there was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel throughout their days.
Baasha king of Israel went to war against Judah and fortified Ramah to prevent anyone from leaving or entering the territory of Asa king of Judah.

So Asa withdrew all the silver and gold that remained in the treasuries of the house of the LORD and the royal palace. He entrusted it to his servants and sent them with this message to Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon, the son of Hezion, the king of Aram, who was ruling in Damascus:

“Let there be a {{berit|treaty}} between me and you as there was between my father and your father. See, I have sent you a gift of silver and gold. Now go and break your treaty with Baasha king of Israel, so that he will withdraw from me.”

And Ben-hadad listened to King Asa and sent the commanders of his armies against the cities of Israel, conquering Ijon, Dan, Abel-beth-maacah, and the whole land of Naphtali, including the region of Chinnereth.
When Baasha learned of this, he stopped fortifying Ramah and withdrew to Tirzah.

Then King Asa summoned all the men of Judah, with no exceptions, and they carried away the stones of Ramah and the timbers Baasha had used for building. And with these materials King Asa built up Geba of Benjamin, as well as Mizpah.

Now the rest of the acts of Asa, along with all his might, all his accomplishments, and the cities he built, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah? In his old age, however, he became diseased in his feet.
And Asa rested with his fathers and was buried with them in the city of his father David, and his son Jehoshaphat reigned in his place.

units.label: Nadab and Baasha reign in Israel
units.note: Ahijah's word against Jeroboam's house fulfilled

verses.text: In the second year of Asa’s reign over Judah, Nadab son of Jeroboam became king of Israel, and he reigned two years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and walked in the way of his father and in his sin, which he had caused Israel to commit.
Then Baasha son of Ahijah of the house of Issachar conspired against Nadab, and Baasha struck him down at Gibbethon of the Philistines while Nadab and all Israel were besieging the city.
In the third year of Asa’s reign over Judah, Baasha killed Nadab and reigned in his place.

As soon as Baasha became king, he struck down the entire household of Jeroboam. He did not leave to Jeroboam anyone who breathed, but destroyed them all according to the {{dabar|word}} that the LORD had spoken through His servant Ahijah the Shilonite,
because of the sins Jeroboam had committed and had caused Israel to commit, and because he had provoked the LORD, the God of Israel, to anger.
As for the rest of the acts of Nadab, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And there was war between Asa and Baasha king of Israel throughout their days.
In the third year of Asa’s reign over Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah became king of all Israel, and he reigned in Tirzah twenty-four years.
And Baasha did evil in the sight of the LORD and walked in the way of Jeroboam and in his sin, which he had caused Israel to commit.

lev.gloss: Heart, the inner person, will, and character. Abijam's heart is explicitly measured against David's and found wanting, while Asa's heart is later called fully devoted to the LORD all his days, even though his reform of the land's worship remained incomplete.

bamah.gloss: High place, an elevated site used for worship, often unauthorized or idolatrous. Asa removes idols and shrine prostitutes yet leaves the high places standing, an honest record that even a genuinely devoted king's reform can remain partial.

berit.gloss: Covenant or treaty. The BSB footnote notes forms of this Hebrew word are usually translated covenant elsewhere; here Asa uses it for a political alliance with Ben-hadad of Aram, a telling reuse of covenant language for a merely human bargain bought with temple silver.

dabar.gloss: Word, matter, or thing spoken. Baasha's slaughter of Jeroboam's entire household comes to pass exactly according to the dabar the LORD had spoken through Ahijah the Shilonite, chapter 14, confirming again that God's word never fails.

basic.context: After Rehoboam's death, chapter 15 moves rapidly through four reigns using the historian's regular pattern, each king's accession dated against the reign of his counterpart in the other kingdom, his mother's name, the length of his rule, and a verdict of right or evil. Abijam reigns briefly in Judah, walking in his father's sins, yet the LORD preserves his son and Jerusalem's strength purely for David's sake. Asa follows and does right, removing shrine prostitutes and even his own grandmother's idol, though the high places remain and war with Baasha of Israel leads him to buy Aram's help with temple treasure rather than seek the LORD directly. Meanwhile in Israel, Nadab is assassinated by Baasha, who then wipes out Jeroboam's entire house, fulfilling Ahijah's prophecy from chapter 14 to the letter.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative using a repeated regnal formula that becomes the standard measuring rod for every king in Kings, it describes real reigns and their real outcomes rather than prescribing exact behavior for believers today. What endures is the pattern itself, faithfulness to the LORD alone as the true measure of a king, God's covenant faithfulness to David's line despite human failure, and the certainty that His spoken judgments come to pass.
basic.originalLanguages: Abijam's lev, heart, is explicitly measured against David's and found wanting, while Asa's lev is later called fully devoted to the LORD all his days, even though the bamah, high places, remain standing under his reign. When pressed by war, Asa proposes a berit, treaty or covenant, with Ben-hadad of Aram, using the same Hebrew root elsewhere reserved for God's covenant with His people, a telling reuse for a merely political alliance. And Baasha's slaughter of Jeroboam's house comes to pass according to the dabar, word, the LORD had spoken through Ahijah.
basic.typology: Asa's wholehearted, if incomplete, devotion anticipates and falls short of the perfect, undivided devotion of Christ, the true Son of David who leaves nothing of idolatry standing. The preservation of David's line purely for the sake of God's promise, despite Abijam's unfaithfulness, points forward to the sure and unconditional foundation of the Davidic covenant that is finally and fully kept in Jesus, the true and greater Son of David.
basic.christ: Asa's heart was fully devoted to the LORD, yet even his best reform left the high places standing, a reminder that no human king perfectly cleanses the land of idolatry. Jesus is the true Son of David whose devotion to the Father is total and whose reign leaves nothing of rival worship standing, John 4:23-24. And where Asa, in crisis, reached for silver, gold, and a foreign king's help rather than trusting the LORD outright, Christ trusted the Father completely, even to the point of the cross, modeling the undivided reliance Asa's reign only partly achieved.
basic.god: God is faithful to His covenant with David, preserving a son and a strong Jerusalem for Abijam's sake even though Abijam himself did not deserve it, purely because of His promise to David. God is also the one whose spoken judgments never fail, as Baasha's brutal rise fulfills exactly what Ahijah foretold chapters earlier. And God delights in wholehearted devotion, honoring Asa's reforms even while the record is honest that they remained incomplete, showing that God receives real, if imperfect, faithfulness without pretending it is perfection.
basic.crossReferences: Second Chronicles 14 to 16 gives a fuller account of Asa's reign, including the prophet Hanani's rebuke of his treaty with Aram. Second Samuel 7 records the LORD's covenant promise to David that underlies why his line is preserved despite Abijam's failure. First Kings 11:29-39 and 14:7-16 record Ahijah's original prophecy against Jeroboam's house, now fulfilled through Baasha. And 2 Chronicles 16 records Asa's later, similar reliance on Aram rather than the LORD, and its consequences.
basic.oneStory: Kings rise and fall in these four reigns, some faithful, some not, but two threads hold steady beneath the change, the LORD keeps His covenant promise to David's house no matter how unfaithful individual kings become, and His spoken word of judgment against Jeroboam's house comes to pass exactly as declared. Both threads point to the same conclusion the whole story is building toward, that only in Christ, the true and perfectly faithful Son of David, is the promise finally, fully, and unshakeably kept.

context.literary: The chapter is organized entirely by the historian's repeated regnal formula, accession synchronism, mother's name, reign length, and moral verdict, a pattern so consistent across Kings that its interruptions, such as the added detail about Asa's specific reforms and his war with Baasha, stand out as deliberately emphasized. The narrative alternates between Judah and Israel, reinforcing that both kingdoms are under the same divine scrutiny even as their fates diverge.
context.historical: Maacah, named as mother to Abijam and grandmother to Asa, likely held the powerful office of queen mother, explaining why her removal from that position, rather than mere personal rebuke, was a significant act of reform. Ben-hadad of Damascus and the Aramean kingdom were a major regional power whose alliance or hostility repeatedly shaped Israelite and Judean politics in this period, and the cities Ben-hadad's forces struck, Ijon, Dan, and the region of Chinnereth, lay along Israel's vulnerable northern frontier. Fortifying Ramah, close to Jerusalem, threatened to strangle Judah's access north and south, explaining the urgency of Asa's response.
context.canonical: This chapter continues the alternating account of Israel and Judah's kings that structures the rest of 1 and 2 Kings, establishing Asa as an early example of a reforming king later echoed by Hezekiah and Josiah, while the fulfillment of Ahijah's word against Jeroboam's house through Baasha reinforces the book's larger argument that every prophetic word concerning these dynasties comes true, whether for judgment or, ultimately in David's line, for the sake of the coming Messiah.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To measure every king, north and south, against the same standard of wholehearted devotion to the LORD modeled by David, to show that God preserves David's line by His own covenant faithfulness rather than any king's merit, and to demonstrate that His prophetic word against Jeroboam's house is fulfilled exactly as spoken.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes specific historical reigns, alliances, and reforms, it does not prescribe political alliances or specific reform measures as a template. What endures is the principle that God evaluates leaders, and every person, by wholehearted devotion rather than partial reform, and that His covenant promises and prophetic words remain utterly reliable regardless of human failure around them.

debates.question: Was Asa's treaty with Ben-hadad of Aram, using temple treasure, a legitimate act of political wisdom or a failure of faith?

views.label: A pragmatic and permissible act of statecraft
views.case: Kings regularly formed alliances for national security, and Scripture does not universally condemn all treaties; Asa acted decisively to relieve real military pressure on Judah, and this chapter itself offers no explicit rebuke.

views.label: A failure to trust the LORD as David once had in crisis
views.case: Second Chronicles 16:7-9 explicitly records the prophet Hanani rebuking Asa for relying on the king of Aram rather than the LORD, saying the LORD's eyes range through the whole earth to strengthen those whose hearts are fully His; read alongside Chronicles, the treaty appears as a lapse into self-reliance.

debates.bearing: Read together with Chronicles, the fuller canonical picture is that Asa's alliance, however militarily effective, represented a step away from the wholehearted trust that marked the best of his reign, a caution against reaching for human solutions before seeking the LORD.

debates.question: Why does the LORD preserve David's line through Abijam despite his unfaithfulness, verses 4-5?

views.label: Unconditional covenant faithfulness to David
views.case: The text explicitly says this happens for the sake of David, recalling the unconditional promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-16 that David's line would endure, a promise not nullified by any single king's failure.

views.label: Preserving the messianic line toward its appointed fulfillment
views.case: The preservation of a lamp in Jerusalem, a recurring image for a continuing dynasty, protects the line that must eventually produce the promised Messiah, so its preservation here serves the larger redemptive storyline rather than rewarding Abijam.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same truth, that God's covenant commitments do not depend on human faithfulness, a pattern that culminates in the certain, unbreakable kingship of Christ, the final Son of David.

study.originalLanguages: Abijam's lev, heart, is explicitly measured against David's and found wanting, while Asa's lev is later called fully devoted to the LORD all his days, even though the bamah, high places, remain standing under his reign. When pressed by war, Asa proposes a berit, treaty or covenant, with Ben-hadad of Aram, using the same Hebrew root elsewhere reserved for God's covenant with His people, a telling reuse for a merely political alliance. And Baasha's slaughter of Jeroboam's house comes to pass according to the dabar, word, the LORD had spoken through Ahijah.

typology.type: David's line preserved for his sake despite Abijam's unfaithfulness
typology.fulfillment: The unconditional Davidic covenant, finally and fully secured in Christ, the eternal Son of David, Luke 1:32-33, 2 Samuel 7:12-16.

typology.type: Asa's genuine but incomplete devotion, leaving the high places standing
typology.fulfillment: Contrasted with Christ's complete and final removal of every rival to true worship, John 4:21-24.

typology.type: Ahijah's word of judgment against Jeroboam's house fulfilled through Baasha
typology.fulfillment: The unfailing certainty of every word God speaks, whether judgment or promise, grounding confidence in all that is promised concerning Christ, Isaiah 55:11.

study.christ: Asa's heart was fully devoted to the LORD, yet even his best reform left the high places standing, a reminder that no human king perfectly cleanses the land of idolatry. Jesus is the true Son of David whose devotion to the Father is total and whose reign leaves nothing of rival worship standing, John 4:23-24. And where Asa, in crisis, reached for silver, gold, and a foreign king's help rather than trusting the LORD outright, Christ trusted the Father completely, even to the point of the cross, modeling the undivided reliance Asa's reign only partly achieved.
study.god: God is faithful to His covenant with David, preserving a son and a strong Jerusalem for Abijam's sake even though Abijam himself did not deserve it, purely because of His promise to David. God is also the one whose spoken judgments never fail, as Baasha's brutal rise fulfills exactly what Ahijah foretold chapters earlier. And God delights in wholehearted devotion, honoring Asa's reforms even while the record is honest that they remained incomplete, showing that God receives real, if imperfect, faithfulness without pretending it is perfection.

crossReferences.note: The unconditional covenant promise to David that explains why his line is preserved despite Abijam's failure.
Gives fuller detail on Asa's reforms and his earlier faith-filled victory over Zerah the Cushite.
Records the prophet Hanani's rebuke of Asa for relying on Aram rather than the LORD in this same conflict.
Ahijah's original prophecy of judgment against Jeroboam's house, now fulfilled through Baasha's slaughter.

study.oneStory: Kings rise and fall in these four reigns, some faithful, some not, but two threads hold steady beneath the change, the LORD keeps His covenant promise to David's house no matter how unfaithful individual kings become, and His spoken word of judgment against Jeroboam's house comes to pass exactly as declared. Both threads point to the same conclusion the whole story is building toward, that only in Christ, the true and perfectly faithful Son of David, is the promise finally, fully, and unshakeably kept.

quiz.q: What is said of Abijam's heart in contrast to David's, verse 3?

options.text: His heart was not as fully devoted to the LORD his God as the heart of David his forefather had been.
options.note: This is the exact statement of verse 3.

options.text: He completely rejected the LORD and worshiped only foreign gods.
options.note: The text says he walked in his father's sins and lacked David's full devotion, not that he rejected the LORD entirely.

options.text: His heart was described as perfect before the LORD.
options.note: The opposite is stated, his heart was not as fully devoted as David's.

options.text: Nothing at all is said about Abijam's heart.
options.note: Verse 3 speaks directly about his heart in contrast to David's.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD still preserve a son for Abijam and strengthen Jerusalem, verses 4-5?

options.text: For the sake of David, because David had done what was right in the LORD's eyes all his days except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite.
options.note: The text explicitly gives this reason, tied to God's promise to David.

options.text: Because Abijam himself had earned it through his own righteousness.
options.note: The text credits David's record and God's promise, not Abijam's own merit.

options.text: Because Israel demanded it be so.
options.note: No such demand from Israel is mentioned; the reason given is God's promise to David.

options.text: The text says the LORD ended David's line at this point.
options.note: The opposite is stated, the LORD preserved the line for David's sake.

quiz.q: What did Asa do that marked him as a king who did right in the LORD's eyes, verses 11-13?

options.text: He banished the male shrine prostitutes, removed the idols his fathers had made, and even removed his own grandmother from her position as queen mother for making an Asherah pole, burning it in the Kidron Valley.
options.note: This summarizes exactly what verses 11-13 record.

options.text: He removed every high place in the land without exception.
options.note: Verse 14 explicitly notes the high places were not removed, so this overstates his reform.

options.text: He allied with Baasha to defeat foreign nations.
options.note: Asa was at war with Baasha, not allied with him.

options.text: He restored the very idols his father had set up.
options.note: Asa removed the idols his fathers had made rather than restoring them.

quiz.q: How did Asa respond to Baasha's fortification of Ramah, and how is that choice significant, verses 18-20?

options.text: He sent temple and palace silver and gold to Ben-hadad of Aram to buy an alliance and break Baasha's pressure, relying on a foreign treaty rather than seeking the LORD directly for deliverance.
options.note: This accurately reflects the text and the concern that Chronicles later raises about this decision.

options.text: He fasted and prayed and the LORD alone drove Baasha back without any human alliance.
options.note: The text records Asa's political and financial alliance with Aram, not solely prayer without human means.

options.text: He surrendered Judah's territory to Baasha without resistance.
options.note: Asa took decisive action through the treaty rather than surrendering.

options.text: He ignored the threat entirely and took no action.
options.note: Asa took clear, deliberate action by sending treasure and a message to Ben-hadad.

quiz.q: How does Baasha's rise and his destruction of Jeroboam's entire house, verses 27-29, relate to earlier chapters?

options.text: It fulfills exactly the word the LORD had spoken through Ahijah the Shilonite against the house of Jeroboam, showing again that God's word never fails.
options.note: Verse 29 explicitly ties this event to the fulfillment of Ahijah's prophecy, chapter 14.

options.text: It shows Baasha acting entirely apart from any word the LORD had spoken.
options.note: The text explicitly connects Baasha's actions to the LORD's earlier spoken word through Ahijah.

options.text: It shows Jeroboam's house continuing to prosper under Baasha's rule.
options.note: The opposite happens, Jeroboam's entire household is destroyed.

options.text: It contradicts what Ahijah had prophesied earlier.
options.note: It fulfills, rather than contradicts, Ahijah's earlier prophecy.

questions: Abijam walked in his father's sins, yet God preserved David's line purely for the sake of His own promise, not Abijam's merit. Where do you rely on God's covenant faithfulness even when your own devotion has been inconsistent?
Asa did right yet left the high places standing. Where in your own life have you removed the obvious sins yet left some lesser compromise untouched?

Under pressure from Baasha, Asa turned to a political alliance and temple treasure rather than seeking the LORD as his father David once did in crisis. Where are you tempted to reach for a practical fix rather than trust God first?

Baasha's brutal rise fulfilled exactly what Ahijah had prophesied chapters earlier. How does seeing God's word fulfilled across such a long span strengthen your confidence in promises He has made to you?
Asa's heart was called fully devoted to the LORD all his days despite his incomplete reforms. What does it look like for you to pursue a whole heart toward God even while growth remains unfinished?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes Abishalom is a variant of Absalom, so Maacah's father is the same Absalom known from the account of David's family.

The BSB footnote notes most Hebrew manuscripts read 'war between Rehoboam and Jeroboam' here, while some Hebrew manuscripts and the Syriac read 'Abijam,' and most Greek texts omit the verse; either reading affirms ongoing war between the two kingdoms across this period.

The BSB footnote notes 'His grandmother's name' renders a Hebrew text that literally reads 'his mother's,' with Maacah understood as Asa's grandmother and the queen mother of the realm, matching verses 2 and 13.
The BSB footnote notes that forms of the Hebrew berit are usually translated covenant elsewhere, though rendered treaty here for Asa's political alliance with Ben-hadad.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 15:1-34. No em dashes or en dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter; hyphens in proper names such as Ben-hadad and Abel-beth-maacah are standard orthography and were retained; none introduced elsewhere in this file.
